passages.explanation: After the uproar in Ephesus, Paul sends for the disciples, encourages them, and leaves for Macedonia. He travels through the region, giving the people much encouragement, and arrives in Greece, where he stays three months. When a Jewish plot against him is discovered as he's about to sail for Syria, he decides to go back through Macedonia instead. Several companions are listed — Sopater, Aristarchus, Secundus, Gaius, Timothy, Tychicus, and Trophimus — from various churches across the mission field. They go ahead and wait at Troas. Paul and Luke sail from Philippi after the Festival of Unleavened Bread and join them in Troas five days later. Luke is narrating in the first person again — he's part of the group.

On the first day of the week, the believers gather to break bread. Paul speaks to them, intending to leave the next day, and keeps talking until midnight. There are many lamps in the upstairs room. A young man named Eutychus is sitting in the window and sinks into a deep sleep as Paul talks on and on. He falls from the third storey and is picked up dead. Paul goes down, throws himself on the young man, and puts his arms around him: don't be alarmed — he's alive! Paul goes back upstairs, breaks bread, eats, and keeps talking until dawn, then leaves. The people take the young man home alive and are greatly comforted. Luke tells this story with understated humour — Paul literally preaches a man to death and then raises him.

Paul's companions sail ahead to Assos, where Paul meets them on foot — he chose to walk. They sail to Mitylene, then past Chios, cross to Samos, and arrive at Miletus. Paul has decided to sail past Ephesus to avoid spending time in the province of Asia, because he's hurrying to reach Jerusalem by Pentecost if possible. He knows a visit to Ephesus would mean a long stay with the believers there. He has a deadline and he's keeping it.

From Miletus, Paul sends for the elders of the Ephesian church. When they arrive, he gives them a farewell speech — the only speech in Acts addressed to a Christian audience. He reminds them how he lived among them the whole time: serving the Lord with humility and tears and trials from Jewish plots. He didn't hesitate to preach anything helpful, teaching publicly and from house to house, declaring to both Jews and Greeks repentance toward God and faith in the Lord Jesus. Now, compelled by the Spirit, he's going to Jerusalem, not knowing what will happen — only that the Holy Spirit warns him in every city that prison and hardships await. But he considers his life worth nothing compared to finishing the race and completing the task the Lord Jesus gave him: testifying to the good news of God's grace. Paul knows he's heading into suffering and goes anyway.

Paul tells them plainly: none of you will ever see me again. He declares himself innocent of everyone's blood — he didn't shrink from declaring the whole will of God. Then he charges them: keep watch over yourselves and the entire flock the Holy Spirit has made you overseers of. Be shepherds of the church of God, which he bought with his own blood. He warns them: after he leaves, savage wolves will come in and won't spare the flock. Even from among their own number, men will arise and distort the truth to draw disciples after themselves. So be on guard — remember that for three years he never stopped warning each of them night and day with tears. This is leadership development in a farewell: protect the flock, watch for internal and external threats, and remember the example I set.

Paul commends them to God and to the word of his grace, which can build them up and give them an inheritance among all who are sanctified. He reminds them he hasn't coveted anyone's silver, gold, or clothing. His own hands supplied his needs and his companions' needs. In everything he showed them that by working hard they must help the weak, remembering Jesus' words: it is more blessed to give than to receive — a saying of Jesus not recorded in the Gospels, preserved here by Paul. When he finishes, he kneels and prays with them all. They all weep, embrace him, and kiss him, grieving most of all because he said they'd never see his face again. They accompany him to the ship. It's one of the most emotionally raw scenes in Acts.
